titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 9 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/214

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
April 9th. My p r Your most thrilling letter was brought by Douglas
early this morning & has been reread over & over again but
will be burnt probably tomorrow ! I a little wish Ed had not asked for that interview
it is just the line I want not to take. I know what
it is in a small way having people anxious to see you at
all hours but only on the smallest scale compared. I don't think I should press for it for him next time
he is up. It really is not fair, tho' I suppose he would say
it is part of his business. So it is I see, but somehow I'd
rather be the other way & not get on. Mater's 25 shall go towards the p r. It is good for her. I can hardly wait for your most wildly exciting packets to
hatch out & feel inclined to bust too watching the Morning post
& Squiffy's attempts to get Sir E into a false position. I
see the traps set for him now, but am disappointed at
Bonar Law who shows that tho' a most sincere patriot he
is not a really great man in the widest sense. A good adminis-
trator but out of touch with the great events which call
for greatness. I'm afraid the summing up of K is correct & it will
be difficult for Sir E to find his collegues perhaps if he does come
in, but the main thing is the Great man at the top
with large ideas, & there are plenty of good administrators
Craig etc I will try to get a definite decision about my leave
tomorrow but I hardly think it will be possible for me
to go now & I shall not press it, much tho' I long to see
my p r again. I do hope she is alright again. Douglas did
not get much time at home & so far I have had little
chance of talking to him. I want to hear about your eyes. I think Place will stand up to the GOC & rather look forward
to the results. Former is tryintaking up many of my suggestions
which were badly sat upon perviously. I don't think there would be any difficulty in your
sharing a houes. If I came home on leave I should probably not
stop with you — in the suburbs ! — more than one night, the remainder
would London or Oxford or Broads. In fact if you still hanker after
a country house & won't feel it makes you too tired travelling I think
a companion might be wise if you started right off on the lines that
you do not attempt to entertain the other. I like to think of the p r with her Duchess & her big man,
it seems quite right somehow. Let me know about your eyes Your b w Wilfrid B Spender
